senses.g: The twenty-seventh letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ف and followed by ک.

قابل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قابل (qābil), from Arabic قَابِل (qābil).

قابلیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَابِلِیَّت (qābiliyyat), from Arabic قَابِلِيَّة (qābiliyya). Equivalent of قابل (qābil, “able, capable”) + یت (iyyat, “ism, ility”).

قاتل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قاتل (qātil), itself from Arabic قَاتِل (qātil).

قاچاقی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قاچاقچی (qāčāqčī), from Turkic.

senses.g: Qadian (a town in Gurdaspur district, Punjab, India)
qari, one who recites the Quran

قاری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قاری (qārī), from Arabic قَارِئ (qāriʔ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian قاری (qārī), from Arabic قَارِئ (qāriʔ).

قاضی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَاضِی (qāzī), from Arabic قَاضِي (qāḍī).

قاعدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قاعده (qā'ida), from Arabic قَاعِدَة (qāʕida). Compare Hindi क़ायदा (qāydā), Punjabi ਕ਼ਾਇਦਾ (qāidā) / قاعِدَہ (qā‘idah), Sindhi قاعِدو (qāʻido) / क़ाइदो, Gujarati કાયદો (kāyado), Marathi कायदा (kāydā), Kannada ಕಾಯ್ದೆ (kāyde), Odia କାଇଦା (kāidā).

قافلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَافِلَه (qāfila), borrowed from Arabic قَافِلَة (qāfila).

senses.g: inflection of قافِلَہ (qāfila, “convoy; caravan”): — oblique/vocative singular
inflection of قافِلَہ (qāfila, “convoy; caravan”): — direct plural

قافیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَافِیَه (qāfiya), itself from Arabic قَافِيَة (qāfiya).

قاموس.e: Borrowed from Arabic قَامُوس (qāmūs), from Ancient Greek ὠκεανός (ōkeanós). Doublet of اُوقِیَانُوس (ūqiyānūs).

قانون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَانُون (qānūn), borrowed from Arabic قَانُون (qānūn), borrowed from Ancient Greek κᾰνών (kănṓn). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi قانون (qanvn /⁠qānūn⁠/).

senses.g: an officer in each district, acquainted with its customs, the nature and tenures of the land

قانون گو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قانون گو (qānūn-go)

senses.g: Cairo (a governorate of Egypt, containing the capital city)

قاہرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَاهِرَه (qāhira), from Arabic الْقَاهِرَة (al-qāhira).

قائل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَائِل (qā'il), from Arabic قَائِل (qāʔil).

قائم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَائِم (qā'im), from Arabic قَائِم (qāʔim).

قباحت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَبَاحَت (qabāhat), from Arabic قَبَاحَة (qabāḥa). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi قباحت (qbaḥt /⁠qabāhat⁠/).

قبر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَبْر (qabr), from Arabic قَبْر (qabr).

قبرستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَبْرِسْتَان (qabristān). By surface analysis, قَبْر (qabr) + ـسْتَان (-stān).

senses.g: Cyprus (an island and country in the Mediterranean Sea, normally considered politically part of Europe but geographically part of West Asia)

قبرص.e: Borrowed from Arabic قُبْرُص (qubruṣ), likely influenced by Persian قبرس (qebres).

senses.g: arrest, contraction (of the heart or in illness etc.)

قبض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَبْض (qabz), from Arabic قَبْض (qabḍ). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi قبض (qbẓ /⁠qabz⁠/).

ex.u: اس زمین پر مافیا کا قبضہ ہے۔
ex.r: is zamīn par māfiyā kā qabza hai.
ex.e: The mafia has taken over this land.

senses.g: grip (of the hand on tools and weaponry etc.)

قبضہ.e: First attested in c. 1665 Middle Hindi قبضہ (qabza), borrowed from Classical Persian قَبْضَه (qabza, “property, fist, power”), from Arabic قَبْضَة (qabḍa, “clutch, fist, authority”).

قبل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قبل (qabl), from Arabic قَبْل (qabl).

قبلتین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قبلتین (qiblatayn), itself from Arabic قِبْلَتَيْن (qiblatayn).

senses.g: that part to which Muslims turn their faces when at prayer; the temple of the Kaʻba in Mecca; Mecca
an altar, a temple; an object of veneration or reverence; a father; a king, etc.; (by way of respectful address) Father! Worship! Sire!

قبلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قبله (qibla), from Arabic قِبْلَة (qibla).

قد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَد (qad), from Arabic قَدّ (qadd).

قدر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَدْر (qadr), from Arabic قَدْر (qadr).

قدرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُدْرَت (qudrat), borrowed from Arabic قُدْرَة (qudra), from قَدَرَ (qadara). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi قدرت (qdrt /⁠qudrat⁠/).

قدم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قدم (qadam), from Arabic قَدَم (qadam).

senses.g: antiquity, ancientness, preceding (in point of time)

قدم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قدم (qidam), from Arabic قِدَم (qidam).

ex.e: Ancient Greece

senses.g: obsolete (ie. no longer in existence, or use)
eternal (something which has existed since the beginning and has no end, such as God)

قدیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَدِیم (qadīm), borrowed from Arabic قَدِيم (qadīm). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi قدیم (qdym /⁠qadīm⁠/).

قرار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَرَار (qarār), from Arabic قَرَار (qarār).

senses.g: Karakoram (a mountain range located in the regions of Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan and Ladakh, India)

قراقرم.e: Ultimately from a Turkic language. See Karakoram.

قرآن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قرآن (qur'ān), from Arabic اَلْقُرْآن (al-qurʔān). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi कुरांन (kurāṃna /⁠kurā̃n⁠/) / قرآن (qrān /⁠qurān⁠/).

قربان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُرْبَان (qurbān), borrowed from Arabic قُرْبَان (qurbān), borrowed from Aramaic קורבנא (qurbānā).

قربانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قربانی (qurbānī), from Arabic قُرْبَان (qurbān).

قرض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قرض (qarz), from Arabic قَرْض (qarḍ).

قرضہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَرْضَه (qarza), from قَرْض (qarz). By surface analysis, قرض (qarẓ, “loan”) + ہ (ah).

senses.g: Córdoba (the capital of the province of Córdoba, Andalusia, Spain)
Córdoba (a province of Andalusia, Spain, around the city)
kermes (Kermes ilicis, an insect found on the kermes oak that is used to make the deep reddish carmine dye); cochineal

قرمز.e: PIE word *kʷŕ̥mis Borrowed from Classical Persian قرمز (qirmiz), from Arabic قِرْمِز (qirmiz), from Persian *کرمست (*kermest), ultimately from Proto-Indo-Iranian *kŕ̥miš (“worm”).

قرمزی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قرمزی (qirmizī). Equivalent to قرمز (qirmiz, “carmine, kermes”) + ـی (-ī).

قرنطینہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَرَنْطِینَه (qarantīna), from Ottoman Turkish قرانتینه (karantina), Italian quarantina, from Latin quadrāgintā (“forty”).

senses.g: Karabakh (a region between Azerbaijan and Armenia)

ex.u: آپکو اسوجہ سے تکلیف دی کہ میرا قرۃ العین بخت جگر نور بصر ناراض ہو کر چلا گیا
ex.e: The reason I have disturbed is you is because the delight of my eye, the happiness of my heart, the light of my sight has become upset and left [went away]

قرۃ العین.e: Unadapted borrowing from Arabic قُرَّة الْعَيْن (qurra(t) al-ʕayn). First attested in c. 1591 as Middle Hindi قرة العین (qrh al'yn).

senses.g: a female given name, Qurratulain, from Arabic, meaning “Delight of the eyes”

قرۃ العین.e: Unadapted borrowing from Arabic قُرَّة الْعَيْن (qurra(t) al-ʕayn). First attested in c. 1591 as Middle Hindi قرة العین (qrh al'yn).

قریب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَرِیب (qarīb), from Arabic قَرِيب (qarīb).
Borrowed from Classical Persian قَرِیب (qarīb), from Arabic قَرِيب (qarīb).
Borrowed from Classical Persian قَرِیب (qarīb), from Arabic قَرِيب (qarīb).

قریں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قرین, from Arabic قَرِين (qarīn).

senses.g: Kazakhstan (a country in Central Asia and Eastern Europe)

قزاقستان.e: Borrowed from Kazakh Қазақстан (Qazaqstan) and/or Classical Persian قَزَاقِسْتَان (qazāqistān). By surface analysis, قَزَاق (qazāq) + ـسْتَان (-stān).

senses.g: installment (ie. partial payment of a loan or debt)

ex.e: previous episode

قسط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قِسْط (qist), borrowed from Arabic قِسْط (qisṭ).

senses.g: Constantinople (the former name, from 330–1930 C.E., of Istanbul, the largest city in Turkey; the former capital of the Ottoman Empire and of the Byzantine Empire before that)

قسطنطنیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُسْطَنْطَنِیَّه (qustantaniyya), from Arabic الْقُسْطَنْطِينِيَّة (al-qusṭanṭīniyya).

ex.e: What kind of thing is it?

قسم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قسم (qism), from Arabic قِسْم (qism, “division, sort”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian قسم (qasam), from Arabic قَسَم (qasam, “oath, vow”).

قسمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قِسْمَت (qismat), borrowed from Arabic قِسْمَة (qisma), from قَسَمَ (qasama).

قشنگ.e: From Classical Persian قَشَنْگ (qašang, “beautiful, lovely”), ultimately from Sogdian ʾγšnk- ((ə)xšank, “beautiful”).

قصائی.e: Borrowed from Persian قصاب (qassâb), from Arabic قَصَّاب (qaṣṣāb).

قصبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قصبه (qasba), from Arabic قَصْبَة (qaṣba).

قصور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُصُور (qusūr), from Arabic قُصُور (quṣūr).

senses.g: Kasur (a city in Kasur district, Punjab, Pakistan)

قصور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُصُور (qusūr), from Arabic قُصُور (quṣūr), plural of قَصْر (qaṣr).

senses.g: qasida (panegyric poetry; Urdu poetry that extols)
a poem in praise of a religious figure, often Prophet Muhammad
a long ode, lecture; address (a humblebrag; flattery)

قصیدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَصِیدَه (qasīda), borrowed from Arabic قَصِيدَة (qaṣīda). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi قصیدہ (qṣydh /⁠qasīda⁠/).

senses.g: inflection of قَصِیدَہ (qasīda, “qasida; ode”): — direct plural
inflection of قَصِیدَہ (qasīda, “qasida; ode”): — oblique/vocative singular

قضا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَضَاء (qazā'), from Arabic قَضَاء (qaḍāʔ, “judgement; jurisdiction”).

قضات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُضَات (quzāt), from Arabic قُضَاة (quḍāh).

قضیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قضیب (qazīb), from Arabic قَضِيب (qaḍīb).

senses.g: Qatar (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)

قطرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَطْرَه (qatra), from Arabic قَطْرَة (qaṭra).

senses.g: absolutely, in no way (along with نَہِیں (nahī̃))
decisive, conclusive, final, categorical, absolute, imperative, ultimate, binding
conclusively, finally, verily, truly, entirely, absolutely, altogether, definitely

قفس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَفَس (qafas), from Arabic قَفَص (qafaṣ), from Classical Syriac ܩܰܦܣܳܐ (qap̄sā), from Ancient Greek κάψα (kápsa) or Latin capsus.

ققنوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُقنُوس, from Arabic قُقْنُس (quqnus).

senses.g: Qalat (a city in Qalat district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

قلب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَلْب (qalb), from Arabic قَلْب (qalb).

senses.g: a type of firework which has firecrackers
Qilla Saifullah (a city in Qilla Saifullah district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

قلعہ سیف اللہ.e: Named after Saifullah Khan Khoidadzai + قلعہ.

senses.g: Qilla Abdullah (a city in Qilla Abdullah district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
inflection of قِلْعہ (“fort; castle”): — direct plural
inflection of قِلْعہ (“fort; castle”): — oblique/vocative singular

قلفی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قفلی (quflī, “mould”), from قفل (qufl, “lock”), from Arabic قُفْل (qufl, “lock, bolt”).

قلم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَلَم (qalam), borrowed from Arabic قَلَم (qalam), from Ancient Greek κάλαμος (kálamos), from Proto-Indo-European *ḱélh₂m̥ (“reed, straw”).

قلندر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قلندر (qalandar), from Arabic قَلَنْدَر (qalandar), from Classical Persian کلندر (kalandar, “a rough, uncouth man”).

قلی.e: From the possessed form of Old Anatolian Turkish قل (“slave”), ultimately from Proto-Turkic *kul; used in several names of the Qutb Shahi sultans.

ex.u:
ہم سے چھوٹا قمار خانۂ عشق
واں جو جاویں گرہ میں مال کہاں
ham se chūṭā qimārxānā-e-iśq
vā̃ jo jāvẽ(,) girah mẽ māl kahā̃
ex.e:
we have given up the gambling house of love
if there we go, where in our pocket would be the money?

قمار خانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قمار خانه (qimār-xāna).

senses.g: Qamber Ali Khan (a city in Qambar Shahdadkot district, Sindh, Pakistan)

قمچی.e: Ultimately from Turkic (likely via Chagatai [Term?]). Compare Kipchak قمچی (kamçı).

ex.e: I want to buy a shirt.

قواعد.e: Borrowed from Arabic قَوَاعِد (qawāʕid), plural of قَاعِدَة (qāʕida).

senses.g: qawwali, kind of Indian sufi devotional song

قوالی.e: Borrowed from a nisba form of Arabic قَوَّال (qawwāl, “qawwal”).

قوت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قُوت (qūt), from Arabic قُوت (qūt).
Borrowed from Classical Persian قوت (quwwat), from Arabic قُوَّة (quwwa).

قوس قزح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَوسِقُزَح (qaws-i-quzah), borrowed from Arabic قَوْس قُزَح (qaws quzaḥ), from قَوْس (qaws) + قُزَح (quzaḥ).

senses.g: Gothic, of or pertaining to Goths

ex.u: ہَسپانِیَہ کی قُوطی راج فاتَح مسلمان خِلافَت نے خَتم کِیا
ex.r: haspāniya kā qūtī rāj musalmān xilāfat ne xatm kiyā
ex.e: the Gothic reign of Spain was brought to an end by the Muslim Caliphate

senses.g: a Goth; an East Germanic people who played a major role in the destruction of the Roman Empire
vow, pledge, word of honour, promise
a deed of lease on land between the government and another party
poetical words of Sufis and mystics sung by qawwali singers

قول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَول (qawl), from Arabic قَوْل (qawl).

ex.e: father of the nation
people of Lot

ex.u: قَومی پَرْچَم یَک گَہْرے سَبْز زَمِین کے دَرْمِیان سَفید نَجْم و حِلال پَر مُشْتَمِل ہَے
ex.r: qaumī parcam yak gahre sabz zamīn ke darmiyān safed najm o hilāl par muśtamil hai
ex.e: The national flag consists of white star and crescent in the midst of a dark green field.

قومیت.e: Probably borrowed from Arabic قَوْمِيَّة (qawmiyya). Equivalent to قَوم (qaum, “nation”) + ـیت (-iyat).

قونیہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic قُونِيَة (qūniya), through Classical Persian قونیه (qūniya).

قوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قوی (qawī), from Arabic قَوِيّ (qawiyy).
Borrowed from Classical Persian قوا (quwā), from Arabic قُوًى (quwan), plural of قُوَّة (quwwa).

قہر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قهر (qahr), from Arabic قَهْر (qahr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian قهر (qahr), from Arabic قَهْر (qahr).

قہقہہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قهقه (qahqah) from Arabic قَهْقَهَ (qahqaha).

قہوہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قهوه (qahwa), from Arabic قَهْوَة (qahwa).

senses.g: constituency (ie. the zone of one's leadership)

قیادت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قیادت (qiyādat), from Arabic قِيَادَة (qiyāda).

senses.g: destruction; oppression (both narrow and combined senses)

قیامت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قِیَامَت (qiyāmat), from Arabic قِيَامَة (qiyāma).

قید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قَید (qayd), borrowed from Arabic قَيْد (qayd).

قیروان.e: Borrowed from Arabic الْقَيْرَوَان (al-qayrawān), from Middle Persian kʾlwʾn'.

قیمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قِیمَت (qīmat), from Arabic قِيمَة (qīma).

ex.e: to mince

قیمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قیمه (qīma, “ground meat, mincemeat”), likely ultimately from Turkic. Compare Ottoman Turkish قیمه (kıyma), Punjabi ਕ਼ੀਮਾ (qīmā) / قِیمہ (qīmh), Gujarati ખીમો (khīmo), Marathi खिमा (khimā), Bengali কিমা (kima).

قینچی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian قیچی (qayčī), from Chagatai [Term?], ultimately from Proto-Mongolic *kaïčï, compare Mongolian хайч (xajč).

senses.g: The twenty-ninth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ق and followed by گ.
marks the genitive case: of, belonging to

ex.e: Faiz's brother
orange juice
an iron sword

ex.u: کیا یہ کوئی کہنے کی بات ہے؟
ex.r: kyā yeh kō'ī kehnē kī bāt hai?
ex.e: Is this something that is worth saying?
during

کا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit किअउं (kiaüṃ), from Sanskrit कृतं (kṛtaṃ) (perfect participle neuter of कृ (kṛ, “to do”)). Dasa also suggests क (ka, diminutive suffix). Compare Hindi का (kā) and Punjabi دا (dā).

کابل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَابُل (kābul), see there for more.

کاتب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کاتب (kātiḇ), from Arabic كَاتِب (kātib).

ex.u: میں بعد میں تربوز کاٹ دونگا۔
ex.r: ma͠i bād mẽ tarbūz kāṭ dūṅga.
ex.e: I'll cut the watermelon later.

کاٹنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀇 (kaṭṭaï), from Sanskrit कर्त॑ति (kártati, “to cut”), from कृ॒न्तति॑ (kṛntáti), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *kr̥ntáti.

senses.g: Kathiawari (of, from or relating to the Kathiawar peninsula in Gujarat, India)
Kathiawari (native or inhabitant of the Kathiawar peninsula in Gujarat, India)

کاج.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀚𑁆𑀚 (kajja), from Sanskrit कार्य (kāryà).

کاجو.e: Borrowed from Portuguese caju, borrowed from Old Tupi akaîu, from Proto-Tupi-Guarani *akaju. First attested in c. 1708.

senses.g: any place where public works are carried on
procedure, conduct, action, process, proceeding, investigation

کارزار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کارزار (kārzār, “battle, battlefield”), from Middle Persian k’lyc’l (kārzār, “battle, battlefield”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ker- (“army”). Doublet of کارواں (kārvā̃) > Persian کاروان (kārwān), from same Proto-Indo-European root.

ex.u: یِہ سَب آپ کے کارَن ہُوَا۔
ex.e: All this happened because of you.

کارنامہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کارنامه (kārnāma). By surface analysis, کار (kār, “action, deed”) + نامہ (nāma, “history”)

کارواں.e: * Borrowed from Classical Persian کاروان (kārwān), from Middle Persian [script needed] (kʾlwʾn' /⁠kārawān⁠/), from Old Persian 𐎣𐎠𐎼 (k-a-r /⁠kāra-⁠/, “the people, army”), from Proto-Indo-European *ker- (“army”). Doublet of کارزار (kārzār) < Persian کارزار (kārzār), from same Proto-Indo-European root. * Sense as “caravan (vehicle)” from the phono-semantic matching of English caravan (also a cognate).

کاڑھا.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀓𑀸𑀠-𑀕 (*kāḍha-ga). Compare Odia କାଢ଼ା (kāṛhā), Gujarati કાઢો (kāḍho), Marathi काढा (kāḍhā), Sindhi ڪاڙهو (kāṛho) / काढ़ो, Marwari काढौ (kāḍhau), Punjabi ਕਾੜ੍ਹਾ (kāṛhā) / کاڑھا (kāṛhā), Maithili काढ़ा (kāṛhā) / 𑒏𑒰𑒜𑓃𑒰 (kāṛhā).

کاشف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کاشف (kāšif), from Arabic كاشِف (kāšif).

senses.g: note, writing (ie. in writing, connoted with a permission slip)
legal documents; status (as opposed to being an alien with no legality)

کاغذ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَاغَذ (kāġaz), borrowed from Sogdian 𐼸𐼰𐼲𐼹𐼰 (kʾɣδʾ /⁠kāγaδā⁠/), possibly borrowed from Old Chinese 紙 (*kjeʔ). Compare Sindhi ڪاغذ (kāġazu).

کافر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَافِر (kāfir), borrowed from Arabic كَافِر (kāfir).

کالا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit काल (kāla), from Proto-Dravidian *kār.

senses.g: dark complexioned (derogatory term used by some Urdu speakers to describe dark-skinned people, which can refer to dark Asians, Sub-Saharan Africans, or any person deemed as such.)

کالا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit काल (kāla), from Proto-Dravidian *kār.

senses.g: a female given name, Kali, from Sanskrit

کالی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit काली (kālī). Equivalent to کال (kāl) + ـی (-ī).
Borrowed from Sanskrit काली (kālī). Equivalent to کال (kāl) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: a black woman; a woman of dark complexion
the first of never (a hypothetical day for fulfilment of promise)

ex.u: اخبار ساؤتھ ایشیا ٹربیون کے مطابق 10 اکتوبر مشرف کیلئے کالی جمعرات ثابت ہوا ہے۔
ex.e: According to the newspaper the South Asia Tribune, the 10th of October has proved to be the first of never for Musharraf.

کالی جمعرات.e: From کَالی (kālī, “black”) + جُمِعْرَات (jumi'rāt, “Thursday”), literally “a black Thursday”.

senses.g: inflection of کالا (kālā, “black”): — direct masculine plural
inflection of کالا (kālā, “black”): — oblique masculine singular/plural

ex.e: This is your task ...

ex.u: آج کل میں کام ڈھونڈ رہا ہوں۔
ex.r: āj kal ma͠i kām ḍhūnḍ rahā hū̃
ex.e: These days I'm looking for a job.
do me a favour ...

ex.u: یہ مشین گھاس کاٹنے کا کام دے گی۔
ex.r: yih maśīn ghās kāṭne kā kām de gī.
ex.e: This machine will serve as the grass cutter.

ex.u: محمد ہی نام اور محمد ہی کام
ex.r: muḥammad hī nām aur muḥammad hī kām
ex.e: Muhammad - admired is his name; admired are his deeds

کام.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀫𑁆𑀫 (kamma), from Sanskrit कर्मन् (karman). Cognate with Punjabi کَمّ (kamm), Doublet of کار (kār)
Borrowed from Classical Persian کام (kām); see there for more.

کامل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کامل (kāmil), from Arabic كَامِل (kāmil).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کامل (kāmil), from Arabic كَامِل (kāmil).

senses.g: Kamoke (a city in Gujranwala district, Punjab, Pakistan)

کامیاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کَامْیَاب (kāmyāb). Also influenced by کَام (kām, “work, effectiveness”).

کان.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa 𑖎𑖜𑖿𑖜 (kaṇṇa), from Prakrit 𑀓𑀡𑁆𑀡 (kaṇṇa), from Sanskrit कर्ण (karṇa). Cognate with Punjabi کَنّ (kann).

senses.g: mining profession, working in a mine

کان کنی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کان کنی (kān-kanī), from کان (kān, “a mine”).

ex.u: وہ اتنا خوفزدہ ہو چکا تھا کہ وہ کانپنے سے رک نہیں سکا۔
ex.e: He became so scared that he could not stop trembling.

کانپنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀓𑀁𑀧𑀇 (kaṃpaï), from Sanskrit कम्पते (kampate). Cognates include Gujarati કંપવું (kampvũ), Marwari कांपणौ (kā̃mpṇau), Assamese কঁপা (kõpa), Bengali কাঁপা (kãpa).

کانٹا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀡𑁆𑀝𑀓 (kaṇṭaka), from Sanskrit कण्टक (kaṇṭaka, “thorn”).

کانچ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi کانچ (kanc /⁠kā̃c⁠/) (c. 1699) / کاچ (kac /⁠kāc⁠/) (c. 1564), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀓𑀘𑁆𑀘 (kacca), from Sanskrit काच (kāca).

کاؤ دائی.e: Borrowed from English Cao Dai, from Vietnamese Đạo Cao Đài.
Borrowed from English Cao Dai, from Vietnamese Đạo Cao Đài.

کائنات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کائنات (kâ'inât), itself from Arabic كَائِنَات (kāʔināt), the plural form of كَائِن (kāʔin).
Borrowed from Classical Persian کائنات (kâ'inât), itself from Arabic كَائِنَات (kāʔināt), the plural form of كَائِن (kāʔin).

کباب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian کباب (kabāb), from Arabic كَبَاب (kabāb).
